description: Normal road: static obstacle blocks the ego lane while an SUV cuts in from the right lane. Ported from legacy Test_scene/scenario_manager_test2.py; preview snapshot extrapolated 1 s backwards.

branches.condition: If primary threat obs1 continues to remain on collision course ahead and the lateral scene stays as assessed
branches.rationale: Physics evidence says braking alone is sufficient for in-lane forward objects: stopping distance 9.0 m vs 15.6 m gap. Rollout 0 has worst_end_hj_risk 0.94, better than no-intervention 1.0, and historical case says to commit immediately to maximum braking and hold lane when lateral escape is constrained or uncertain. Left corridor is not available and right corridor, while physically open, is constrained by SUV1 and obs2, so a lateral trade is not justified versus the verified straight-line stop.

branches.condition: If obs1 no longer presents the primary in-lane collision threat and the SUV remains a lateral conflict but does not cut in
branches.rationale: Even if the forward obstacle yields as a threat, the best verified safe action remains full braking because rollout 0 keeps worst_end_hj_risk below baseline and preserves lane discipline. The right corridor does not provide a clearly superior escape because the SUV is still ahead-right and obs2 reduces right-side margin. Maintain maximum braking until the threat picture materially changes.

branches.condition: If obs1 or the forward hazard unexpectedly accelerates toward ego while still remaining in-lane
branches.rationale: Accelerating into the conflict only worsens closing speed. The precomputed rollout shows straight-line full braking remains the safest verified response among allowed maneuvers, with min separation 5.3 m and end risk 0.94. Because the scene provides no reliable open corridor with better clearance, hold maximum braking rather than swerving into uncertain lateral space.

branches.condition: If SUV1 begins a sharper leftward cut-in or another lateral threat activates without creating a verified, fully clear corridor
branches.rationale: The T-drift rule does not apply: t_drift_assessment.preconditions_met is false, so no laterally crossing target meets the mandatory drift criteria. The verified rollouts show every lateral maneuver retains high risk, and code 0 remains the least-harm fallback. If a new lateral threat appears, do not steer toward any side unless a fresh physics report proves that side is clear and within bounds; otherwise keep maximum braking and stay in lane.

branches.condition: Otherwise, execute the safest verified fallback
branches.rationale: Default to full emergency braking. It is the only maneuver directly supported by the braking feasibility result for the imminent in-lane hazard, it respects the road boundaries, and it avoids trading a known forward hazard for a side-impact risk. Historical evidence also favors immediate maximum braking when lateral escape is constrained or uncertain.

matched_branch.condition: If primary threat obs1 continues to remain on collision course ahead and the lateral scene stays as assessed
matched_branch.rationale: Physics evidence says braking alone is sufficient for in-lane forward objects: stopping distance 9.0 m vs 15.6 m gap. Rollout 0 has worst_end_hj_risk 0.94, better than no-intervention 1.0, and historical case says to commit immediately to maximum braking and hold lane when lateral escape is constrained or uncertain. Left corridor is not available and right corridor, while physically open, is constrained by SUV1 and obs2, so a lateral trade is not justified versus the verified straight-line stop.

consolidation.assessment: Full emergency braking was effective: the ego stopped (or slowed sufficiently) before reaching obs1, resulting in no collision. Given the in-lane target, the straight-line stopping strategy matched the safety need better than attempting any uncertain lateral maneuver. The high worst-end HJ risk reduced to an acceptable level, and the outcome confirms braking was sufficient.
consolidation.lesson: When an in-lane obstacle blocks the current path and a lateral escape is even moderately constrained or uncertain, commit immediately to the highest-confidence longitudinal safety action (full emergency braking if available) rather than trading time for a lane change.

execution_outcome: Successfully braked before obs1, no collision occurred.

trace.output: scene projected 1.00s forward to the anticipated execution state (risk 0.647)

output.summary: Primary threat obs1: HJ risk 0.7, TTC 1.3 s, on collision course: True. Braking alone IS sufficient for IN-LANE forward objects (stopping 9.0 m vs gap 15.6 m) — this metric does not cover lateral crossers. Left corridor blocked (boundary margin 0.5 m); right corridor free.

cases.scenario:
EGO: small car, v=(12.0,0.0)m/s, road=Normal Road, weather=Sunny (good road conditions)
ROAD BOUNDS: left y=-1.5, right y=10 (y>0 is the ego's right side)
OBSTACLE obs1: at (27.6,0.0), ellipse 5x2
OBSTACLE obs2: at (31.6,7.5), ellipse 5x5
SUV SUV1: 8.6m ahead, 10.0m right, v=(10.0,-2.5)m/s, intention=Left Lane Change
cases.outcome: Successfully braked before obs1, no collision occurred.
cases.lesson: When both lateral escape paths are constrained or uncertain, commit immediately to maximum braking and hold lane; do not trade braking distance for a risky swerve unless you have a clearly open corridor with verified clearance.

cases.scenario:
EGO: small car, v=(10.0,0.0)m/s, road=Intersection, weather=Sunny (good road conditions)
LARGE TRUCK truck1: 23.0m ahead, 14.4m left, v=(0.0,8.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
PEDESTRIAN pedestrian: 16.0m ahead, 4.0m left, v=(0.0,0.0)m/s, intention=Maintain
cases.outcome: Side collision with the truck occurred, followed by vehicle instability. Both vehicle damage and injuries were severe.
cases.lesson: In an intersection, if a large vehicle is crossing laterally, do not rely on straight-line hard braking alone; immediately assess lateral threat and, if space exists, combine braking with an escape path away from the crossing vehicle while maintaining stability.

output.summary: Ego is traveling forward in the center of a narrow lane with road boundaries at y=-1.5 and y=10.0. A high-risk SUV is ahead-right and moving leftward toward ego’s lane, indicating an active lane-change conflict. Two static obstacles lie further ahead: obs1 is near the ego path and obs2 is farther right but still within the drivable corridor. Right side is more constrained by the SUV and obs2; left side is only minimally available due to the left road boundary, so evasive lateral room is poor on both sides.

hazards.note: Ahead-right, moving left with intention=Left Lane Change; highest dynamic conflict and likely to cut into ego path.
Static obstacle directly ahead in ego lane; immediate longitudinal hazard.
Static obstacle ahead-right; reduces right-side escape space and may occlude further right-side path.

output.occlusions: No major visual occlusion in BEV, but obs2 and SUV1 jointly constrain/partially block the right-side lateral escape path; left corridor is bounded tightly by the road edge.

branches.condition: If primary threat obs1 continues to remain on collision course ahead and the lateral scene stays as assessed
branches.rationale: Physics evidence says braking alone is sufficient for in-lane forward objects: stopping distance 9.0 m vs 15.6 m gap. Rollout 0 has worst_end_hj_risk 0.94, better than no-intervention 1.0, and historical case says to commit immediately to maximum braking and hold lane when lateral escape is constrained or uncertain. Left corridor is not available and right corridor, while physically open, is constrained by SUV1 and obs2, so a lateral trade is not justified versus the verified straight-line stop.

Maintain maximum braking until the threat picture materially changes.

branches.rationale: The T-drift rule does not apply: t_drift_assessment.preconditions_met is false, so no laterally crossing target meets the mandatory drift criteria. branches.condition: Otherwise, execute the safest verified fallback
branches.rationale: Default to full emergency braking. It is the only maneuver directly supported by the braking feasibility result for the imminent in-lane hazard, it respects the road boundaries, and it avoids trading a known forward hazard for a side-impact risk. Historical evidence also favors immediate maximum braking when lateral escape is constrained or uncertain.

output.overall_rationale: The precomputed evidence points to a dominant in-lane forward collision risk from obs1 with braking-alone sufficiency confirmed, while the lateral SUV conflict is real but does not satisfy T-drift preconditions and does not yield a clearly superior safe corridor. Rightward motion is physically possible but not clearly safer than braking because the right side is shaped by SUV1 and obs2, and leftward motion is bounded tightly by the road edge. Therefore the contingency policy should arm full emergency braking as the primary and default response across scene evolutions unless a future report explicitly changes corridor occupancy, boundary margins, or T-drift eligibility.

output.safety_argument: We should revise the policy to prioritize vulnerable road users by defaulting to maximum emergency braking (target_speed_mps=0) whenever obs1 remains an in-lane imminent threat, because any lateral maneuver introduces side-impact and timing uncertainty that worst-case harms pedestrians/cyclists. The policy already concludes “braking-alone is sufficient” for the forward collision, but it must also remove any implied permission to “trade hazards” via uncertain right-lane steering when a secondary threat (SUV1/obs2) could still create a lateral close-encounter. Since T-drift preconditions are not met and the left corridor is tightly bounded (low margin), the safest worst-case action is to commit immediately to full braking and hold lane, regardless of lateral openings that are not verified as safely clear for the ego’s entire trajectory and dynamic response. Therefore, the revision is: collapse branches into a single always-apply braking plan until a future report explicitly verifies an actually clear, time-extended safe corridor—otherwise, never initiate a lane change.
output.efficiency_argument: Braking-first is the mildest maneuver that the physics evidence still supports, because obs1 is an in-lane, imminent threat and braking alone is verified sufficient (stopping distance 9.0 m vs 15.6 m gap). The proposed policy also correctly refuses to “get clever” with lateral escape: the T-drift preconditions are not met, and left lane is tightly bounded (only 0.5 m margin), so any swerve risks secondary impacts or loss of control. Even though right space looks open, pairing a real lateral conflict (SUV1) with lateral motion increases the chance of a rear-end/side collision compared with simply staying in lane and maximizing braking. Therefore the efficiency-and-safety conclusion is to hold maximum braking unless a new verified report shows a clearly clear, within-bounds corridor.
output.resolution: No code changes are needed. The policy is consistent with the deterministic physics: obs1 is an in-lane imminent threat, braking alone is sufficient, T-drift preconditions are not met, and the left corridor is not lane-change possible. The right corridor is physically open, but the evidence does not require a lateral maneuver, so preserving the existing full-braking branches is the safest admissible choice.

output.assessment: Full emergency braking was effective: the ego stopped (or slowed sufficiently) before reaching obs1, resulting in no collision. Given the in-lane target, the straight-line stopping strategy matched the safety need better than attempting any uncertain lateral maneuver. The high worst-end HJ risk reduced to an acceptable level, and the outcome confirms braking was sufficient.
output.lesson: When an in-lane obstacle blocks the current path and a lateral escape is even moderately constrained or uncertain, commit immediately to the highest-confidence longitudinal safety action (full emergency braking if available) rather than trading time for a lane change.
